topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom felt thick and suffocating, each forced laugh echoing like a chipped piece of glass in my ears. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute so tightly my knuckles were white, a desperate attempt to anchor myself to the present moment that was rapidly dissolving into a sea of forced pleasantries. Then, across the crowded dance floor, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. It was him. He was laughing, his head tilted back, looking impossibly happy, and the sight sent a sharp, unwelcome jolt of something cold through my chest. I immediately lowered my gaze, focusing intently on the intricate pattern of the carpet, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole. Every step I took felt heavy, weighted down by the sheer effort of pretending I wasn't acutely aware of his presence, or the gaping chasm that separated us now.

[story 2]

I navigated the periphery of the reception, a ghost drifting through the glittering throng. The music pulsed with an aggressive cheerfulness that grated on my nerves. My shoulders were hiked up near my ears, a physical manifestation of the tension coiling in my gut. I was trying to look engaged, nodding vaguely at conversations about future plans and successful careers, but my eyes kept flicking toward the entrance. And there he was. He looked radiant, effortlessly charming, the kind of easy joy I distinctly remembered being mine, and the memory twisted something tight and painful in my throat. When our eyes met for a fleeting second, the smile he offered was warm, and it felt like a deliberate, cruel spotlight illuminating all the things I was trying so hard to keep hidden. I quickly turned away, the sudden rush of cold air against my skin the only thing that momentarily cleared my head.

[story 3]

The ambient noise of the celebration was a relentless assault. I stood near the buffet, pretending to examine a platter of canapés, but my attention was entirely fixed on the doorway. Every time the door swung open, my breath hitched, a shallow, involuntary catch in my lungs. There he was, standing there, surrounded by people who clearly adored him. He looked so completely settled, so undeniably content, and the reality of that contentment hit me like a physical blow. I felt a distinct tightening around my jaw, a stubborn refusal to let my expression betray the churning unrest inside. I focused on the condensation dripping down my water glass, counting the drops, anything to avoid the inescapable reality of seeing him again, under these bright, unforgiving lights.
